This was such a weird doc.

They took a pretty serious event that happened, like, this cruise company intentionally cut corners for safety and downplaying to the media, and made it this weird and wacky doc... which focused way too much on the toilet situation?? And honestly, most of the interviewees were so grating and irritating, most especially the group of girls and the weird fiancé guy (I liked some of the staff and the father daughter, actually felt bad for them).

Felt like so much potential for a good doc was wasted, especially just after watching the titan doc as well!

If anyone is reading this and isn't sure if they should watch this, I would bet there is a better doc on youtube somewhere else!

video_title: Trainwreck: Poop Cruise | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
I just watched this documentary yesterday and the one I blame the most is the captain of that ship. He should’ve had his crew members remove the toilets from some of the lower decks and and reinstall the them on the upper deck where the people could do their business off the side. That way you’re not exposed to it nor can you smell it and whatever environmental impact it would have had would have been minuscule at best.

You could’ve had 20 toilets on one side of the upper deck of the ship and 20 on the other with partitions covering them for privacy. One side could have been the ladies room and the other side the men’s and everything would have been just fine. Whoever the captain of that boat was, I wouldn’t so much as have him pilot a radio controlled boat!

video_title: Trainwreck: Poop Cruise | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
There were quite a few funny moments in this documentary - and that chef was hilarious! However, I do wish the documentary covered some of the real dangers the passengers faced - as well as the potential dangers they were lucky enough to avoid. As bad as things were, things could've gotten MUCH worse for them. This doc mainly focused on the passengers who were horrified over the inconveniences they faced .... but what about the passengers who felt vulnerable and afraid because they recognized the potential for tragedy on a drifting, incapacitated ship?

Imagine being stuck on a cruise ship after knowing there'd been a large fire onboard - and then losing access to electricity, running water, and the ability to communicate with the outside world. Surely many of these passengers had worries that went beyond toilet access and the lack of air conditioning. What if a secondary fire broke out on the ship? If so, could it even be extinguished? Were the fire pumps hooked up to emergency generators - or did they require power from the ship's engines to work? Hopefully, it was the former because without working pumps to deliver water to fire hoses or the sprinkler system, there'd be no way to keep a fire from spreading through a ship and consuming everything in its path. Sure, these people were on a floating city surrounded by water, but all that water would've been useless if the pumps didn't work. Also, the ship was listing to one side, so did that mean the (bilge?) pumps that would ordinarily kept the ship from taking on too much water weren't functioning? Surely there were passengers on board who were very concerned about the ship capsizing. For those people, getting free booze was probably the last thing they cared about.

Being stuck on a floating, tilting petri dish/potential firetrap with a finite supply of unspoiled food and drinking water should've been the real nightmare for these people - not the indignity of having to relieving oneself in a receptacle other than a toilet. (That would've been the least of my worries if I'd been a passenger!) Making the documentary about petty, spoiled Americans who refused to poop in bags and had obviously never faced a moment of hardship in their lives detracted from the seriousness of the situation they were in. Just because there were no icebergs in sight didn't mean there was no danger. These passengers may have thought they were stuck in a "worst case scenario", but things absolutely could've gotten much, much worse for them. They're lucky no other fires broke out on the ship - or they didn't get stuck in a violent storm - or there weren't 20+ foot waves beating their (already listing) ship and threatening to capsize it - or the ship didn't drift into shallower waters where a reef could've torn a hole in its hull.

video_title: Trainwreck: Poop Cruise | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
Please do another documentary on this. It felt incomplete and more like a news story in long form. Nothing new was learned that wasn't reported 12 years ago. 
How did the company end up in this situation? 
What caused the fire? Was it an installer error? Manufacturer error? User error? Sabotage? 
What changes did they make to avoid the cause of the fire? 
What backup plan did they create as a result of what happened? 
What provisions have been made for the worst-case scenario? 
What types of pre-cruise safety checks have been implemented as a result?
Have there been any other cruise lines, boats, or ships affected by this same issue? 
What type of emergency planning has been created as a result of this incident?
Were there any medical emergencies? If so, how were they handled? 
So many unanswered. 
Netflix could have kept this documentary "in the oven" a little longer.

video_title: Trainwreck: Poop Cruise | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
“The Poop Cruise: Carnival’s Curse”
They boarded bright with hearts so light,
A sea-bound dream, a sheer delight.
From Galveston to sunlit shore,
A holiday they’d long implore.

But fate would spark a darkened doom,
An engine fire, a metal tomb.
The power failed, the lights went black,
And slowly drifted they — no track.

No fans, no flush, no gourmet spread,
Just bags of red near every bed.
With sewage swells and stench profuse,
They named the ship the Poop Cruise.

No jokes could mask the raw disgrace,
As faeces spread through every place.
Cold buns and queues for meagre aid,
The promised luxury decayed.

They slept like castaways on deck,
The tropic heat, a steaming wreck.
Some wept, some cursed, some simply prayed
For rescue ships that were delayed.

The nightmare lasted five long days,
Through fetid air and soiled malaise.
Till tugboats came to end the blight,
And dragged them slow to dawn’s first light.

Now Netflix shows the tale anew,
But passengers say, “If you only knew…”
For no screen truly can produce
The horrors of the Carnival Poop Cruise.

video_title: Trainwreck: Poop Cruise | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: On day 2 of their ordeal the Liberty came to provide food and such. They mentioned that evacuating the Triumph to the Liberty was impractical because of the logistics of using the small dinghy to ferry a handful of people at a time. My question is, why didn't they have the ships basically trade lifeboats? Fill the Triumph's LB's with all the passengers and non-essential crew, have Liberty drop all her LB's and raise the Triumph's full LB's in their place. Boom, you've moved everyone from one ship to the other in probably a little less than 2 hours. Yeah, the Liberty would be over capacity, but they can get to land in a day and unload. That, imo, is a much better plan than leaving 4200+ people on a ship w/o bathrooms, food, aircon, etc. for an *additional* 4 days.

video_title: Trainwreck: Poop Cruise | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: I was on this cruise and I'm glad to see some of my footage made the film, except, they didn't give credit. To answer some questions I see, Netflix had to stick to their theme. They chose people to interview who would also stick to their theme about it just being about poo. They left out a lot. The worst part was the danger and Carnival didn't tell us much at all while on the ship. We had no idea if everyone survived the fire or not and many people weren't getting food. People mostly slept under the lifeboats because Carnival was not being honest and we listed the entire time (tilted ship). While all this was happening, we were getting different stories from the crew ect. When situations like this happen, a lot of it is uncertainty. It's easy to watch this and know everything that's going on because they are telling you, but in the moment, we had no idea, we woke up to smoke everywhere and knew there was a serious fire and Carnival was lying left and right...that makes a very intense situation. The problem with pooing in bags was that we also didn't have running water until the last few days of 5. So it was go without washing your hands or anything for that matter, or don't go.

comment_text: When they first mentioned tugboats to pull ship to Mexico, I wondered can tug boats really pull a huge cruise ship? You paid for a holiday and it turned into a disaster sometimes things happen in life and when you get home, safe safely get your money refunded.  the person who thought it was funny to throw the red bag overboard when it came back too bad it didn’t hit them in the face.  True character comes out during disaster and difficult situations.

video_title: Trainwreck: Poop Cruise | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
I've been in plumbing for almost 20 years. In condo buildings main sewer/kitchen drain clogs back up to the lowest point. So if you're on the 3rd floor and use water it backs into the units home (Shower/tub for sewer or kitchen sink if kitchen) on the 1st or 2nd (if there's no 1st floor unit) floor unit. A tier typically has 3-6 units on it where I'm at. The amount of people that get flooded AFTER units are informed of the issue is staggering.

So there's no surprise to me that thousands of people on a ship would have this kind of issue. It doesn't take that many selfish assholes to ruin things in a crisis.

video_title: Trainwreck: Poop Cruise | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: I was on this cruise ship the week before. This event didn't have to happen. Carnival should have taken this ship out to be serviced, but money would have been lost on their end doing that. This ship was having problems the day before the crusie we got emails that we would be getting a credit towards our cost for numerous reasons. The ship arrival delay, ship departure delay, and only going to one port Progreso). They provided the reason was electrical issues. On my cruise, the ship was struggling to make it across the gulf. I remember looking over the water from the deck thinking we could swim faster than the boat was moving. I truly feel for these people and what they went through. I have gone on cruises since and always make sure to get a balcony towards the top just in case this happens again.
